titus_atticus_convo_2_n01.npcDialogue: I'm leaving Rome. Don't give me that look — it isn't flight, it's reallocation. Athens has libraries, civilized taxation, and a marked deficiency of senators stabbing each other over breakfast. I've been liquidating for three weeks. The villa in Epirus is aired out, the ships are engaged, my agents have their standing orders. But I didn't invite you here to discuss my itinerary. I've been turning over a question — what becomes of the people who stay behind in a city that's forgetting how to be a city.

responses.playerDialogue: You've been planning this for months — which means you've already planned for the people you're leaving behind. What's the arrangement?
I'm staying. Someone has to be present when the rubble settles, and I intend to be standing on top of it.
Running. I confess I expected something different from you, Atticus.
I don't blame you — this city is becoming something I barely recognize. What counsel would you leave for those of us who remain?

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: Athens. Well — I suppose someone ought to preserve Roman culture somewhere it won't get a knife in its ribs.

titus_atticus_convo_2_n02.npcDialogue: Here is what I can do from Athens — and it is not nothing. The network stays intact: agents in every significant port, contacts threaded through every faction. Information will still flow through me; it will merely flow at the speed of a letter rather than a conversation. More importantly — I've constructed what you might call an insurance policy. If the wind shifts badly for you — proscription lists, exile, property seizure, any of the usual Roman festivities — I can extract you. Ships, safe houses, letters of introduction to the right Greeks. It won't rescue your career, but it will rescue your breathing. The question is whether you want that net beneath you, or whether you'd rather burn it for a stronger position now.

options.playerDialogue: I'll take the escape route. A man who can't survive can't win anything worth winning.
No. Keep your insurance — if I hedge now I'll never fully commit, and half-measures will kill me faster than any proscription list.

titus_atticus_convo_2_n03.npcDialogue: Expected more. Let me tell you about expectations. I expected the Republic to outlast me. I expected senators to settle their quarrels with oratory rather than edged iron. I expected that a man could be wealthy and literate and uninvolved in other men's blood feuds. Every one of those expectations defaulted, so I revised my position. That's not weakness — it's what numerate people do when the data changes. But since you're resolved to stay in this beautiful, hemorrhaging city — let me offer you something practical. I've prepared contingencies. If things break badly — and my models give it seven chances in ten — I can either help you survive the aftermath, or leave you to your convictions. The choice, as always, is yours.

options.playerDialogue: That's fairly argued. Tell me about these contingencies.
Keep your contingencies. Planning for disaster is a way of inviting it through the door.

titus_atticus_convo_2_n04.npcDialogue: Prudent. Not heroic, but prudent — and I have always found prudence more durable than heroism. Here is how it works: if things collapse, send word to my agent at the docks in Puteoli. The phrase is 'Attic shipment.' He'll understand. There will be a berth, documents, and enough coin to establish yourself in Greece until the situation clarifies. It isn't free — consider it a debt between friends, payable when Rome remembers what civilization looks like. Which she will. Eventually. Romans are insufferable, but they are not, in the final accounting, stupid.

options.playerDialogue: 'Attic shipment.' I'll remember. Thank you, Atticus — and I mean that without ornament.

titus_atticus_convo_2_n05.npcDialogue: No net. Full exposure. I'd call it admirable if I didn't also call it reckless — but then, the men who reshape things are usually the ones too obstinate to run from them. What holds you here? Is it duty — that old Roman infection — or ambition? I'm not judging, Crispus. I simply want to understand what sort of man I'm saying goodbye to.

options.playerDialogue: Duty. Rome made me. I won't leave her when she needs what I can give.
Ambition. When powerful men flee, they leave power lying in the street. I intend to pick it up.

titus_atticus_convo_2_n06.npcDialogue: Don't thank me — gratitude makes me deeply uneasy. It implies I acted selflessly, and I promise you my motives are entirely selfish. I want interesting people to survive this catastrophe, because Athens without good conversation is just expensive scenery. Write to me when things settle. Or when they don't — either way I'll want to know the story. Goodbye, friend. I don't use that word often. I use it even less carelessly.

titus_atticus_convo_2_n07.npcDialogue: Duty. The most Roman of answers — and therefore the most dangerous. Men who serve Rome out of duty are the ones she devours and forgets. But they're also the only ones she bothers to remember, centuries later, when the marble goes up. I'll watch from Athens and hope you prove the cynics wrong. You won't have my safety net, but you'll carry something the careful men never earn — conviction. That's worth more than I know how to price. And I can price anything.

titus_atticus_convo_2_n08.npcDialogue: Ambition. Honest ambition, openly worn — rarer than you'd imagine. Most ambitious men dress it in duty or principle or some other respectable toga. You wear it bare. I can work with that, even from across the Adriatic. Ambitious men are predictable, and predictable men are safe to invest in. No escape route, then — but keep my name in your calculations. When you're powerful — if you're powerful — you'll want a banker who isn't frightened of you. There are astonishingly few of us.

deltas.reason: Secured Atticus's escape network as insurance against political catastrophe

effects.reason: Atticus's escape route reduces the consequences if a power shift goes badly

deltas.reason: Refused the safety net and chose duty to Rome over self-preservation
Refused the safety net to seize opportunities left by those who fled
